coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) has emerged as a predominant, highly sensitive, and homogeneous optical analytical technique in the realm of analytical testing and bio-imaging. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) demonstrate a size-dependent, broader absorption range within visible wavelengths owing to the phenomenon of surface plasmon resonance. As a result, they can effectively act as light acceptors, enabling the creation of a donor-acceptor system crucial for achieving precise target analyte analysis. In this comprehensive review, we present an extensive survey of recent research advancements in the field of FRET techniques based on AuNPs for the analytical detection of a wide range of entities, including some biomolecules, pesticides, enzymes, microorganisms, food safety and environmental pollutants. Additionally, we elucidate the procedural strategies and underlying mechanisms involved. Finally, we provide perspectives on the current issues and future efforts surrounding the FRET applications of AuNPs in biological analysis. Overall, this review aims to provide a holistic comprehension of gold nanoparticle applications in life analysis using FRET, while also presenting a promising vision for future endeavors in this domain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) has emerged as a predominant, highly sensitive, and homogeneous optical analytical technique in the realm of analytical testing and bio-imaging. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) demonstrate a size-dependent, broader absorption range within visible wavelengths owing to the phenomenon of surface plasmon resonance. As a result, they can effectively act as light acceptors, enabling the creation of a donor-acceptor system crucial for achieving precise target analyte analysis. In this comprehensive review, we present an extensive survey of recent research advancements in the field of FRET techniques based on AuNPs for the analytical detection of a wide range of entities, including some biomolecules, pesticides, enzymes, microorganisms, food safety and environmental pollutants. Additionally, we elucidate the procedural strategies and underlying mechanisms involved. Finally, we provide perspectives on the current issues and future efforts surrounding the FRET applications of AuNPs in biological analysis. Overall, this review aims to provide a holistic comprehension of gold nanoparticle applications in life analysis using FRET, while also presenting a promising vision for future endeavors in this domain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is an established optical technique that has garnered significant attention from researchers due to its facile operation, heightened sensitivity, excellent specificity, and favorable biocompatibility [1]. It has emerged as a promising tool for quantitative detection of biomolecules and bio-imaging in vivo or in vitro. Consequently, FRET has found widespread utility across diverse fields, including but not limited to food science, medicine, and scientific research [2].

The FRET process entails the utilization of a pair of fluorescent substances. The basic concept involves an energy donor and an acceptor, where the donor transfers its energy to the acceptor when they are in close proximity (usually less than 10 nm) (as shown in Fig. 1 a). This transfer process is influenced by the spectral overlap between the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption, as well as the distance between them. When the absorption spectrum of the acceptor fluorescent substance coincides with the emission spectrum of the donor fluorescent substance (as illustrated in Fig. 1b), while in its excited state, the donor transfers some or all of its energy to the acceptor in a non-radiative manner, thus inducing excitation (as depicted in Fig. 1c). After the energy transfer, the fluorescence intensity of the donor diminishes or is completely extinguished, while the acceptor undergoes enhanced fluorescence or forms a non-luminescent ground state complex with the acceptor molecule. Within this framework, the construction of a donor-acceptor system plays a pivotal role in the detection mechanism. Meanwhile, it is crucial to consider the energy overlap and the high quantum yield of the fluorophore as essential factors when designing the detection system. In general, the materials conventionally used as fluorescent donors consist of organic small molecules dyes [3], quantum dots (QDs) [4], inorganic up conversion fluorescent nanoparticles [5,6], and carbon nanomaterials [7]. The fluorescent acceptors mainly refer to organic small molecule dyes [8], carbon nanomaterials [9], noble metal nanomaterials, and others of such ilk [10]. As a promising biomedical material, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have demonstrated commendable biocompatibility, stability, and optical properties that are contingent upon their size and morphology. As good FRET materials, AuNPs possess the following advantages: Firstly, the optical characteristics can be fine-tuned by adjusting the particle size and morphology [11]. Secondly, their remarkable stability and easily modifiable surface provide an adaptable platform that readily accommodates a wide range of organic or biological fluorescent ligands. Thirdly, AuNPs exhibit a remarkable molar extinction coefficient and a wide absorption band within the visible spectrum, as evidenced by a wealth of research reports [12], rendering them a favored choice for fluorescence quenching reagents in FRET applications. Herein, diverse AuNPs were utilized to develop FRET biosensors catering to varying target analyses.

AuNPs have exhibited distinctively tunable optical capabilities and excellent biocompatibility and stability, rendering them promising candidates for utilization in the realm of biosensors and analytical chemistry. Despite several review articles delving into the application of FRET in biosensors and analytical chemistry, a comprehensive review focusing on the mechanism of FRET in biological analysis based on AuNPs is seldom reported. a comprehensive review focused on the FRET mechanism based on AuNPs to achieve analytical and detection of targets is warranted. Given this, this review primarily aims to elucidate the recent progress in AuNPs-based FRET biosensors, specifically in their analytical applications in the fields of life health and safety. Furthermore, the application prospects and challenges will also be reviewed in this review, and include our optimistic visions and reflections on the future applications of AuNPs-based FRET biosensors in the field of biological analysis.

Fluorescent molecules are capable of being excited by light energy to a higher energy state, which will return to the ground state through radiative relaxation, accompanied by a fluorescence signal [13]. In the presence of a suitable acceptor, the energy of fluorescent molecules will be transferred to the acceptor molecule by FRET [14]. Firstly, suitable wavelength difference between the donor fluorescence and acceptor absorption are the key factors affecting FRET efficiency, which is significantly decreased with increasing the molecular distance [15–18]. In addition, quantum yield, extinction coefficient, water solubility and anti-interference performance are also the considerations for the development of FRET systems. Currently reported organic fluorophores, fluorescent proteins, quantum dots and carbon dots show highly emissive properties, easy to decoration, good stability and biocompatibility, making them suitable as energy donors [19–22]. Meanwhile, AuNPs are widely used as good quenchers for FRET due to their high extinction coefficients and broad absorption spectra that overlap with the emission spectra of many organic dyes and quantum dots [23,24].

At present, there are mainly several experimental mechanisms based on the application of AuNPs in FRET. First, direct quenching method. In this method, AuNPs are directly close to the fluorescent donor molecule, and the donor fluorescence is quenched through the FRET effect, which is adapted to detect molecules that can directly interact with AuNPs. Second, indirect quenching method. The target molecule is utilized as a bridge to connect the fluorescence donor and AuNPs. When the target molecule binds to the donor and acceptor, the FRET efficiency increases, leading to quenching of the donor fluorescence. Third, the competition method. In this experimental design, a fluorescent donor and a molecule (e.g., an aptamer or antibody) that binds to AuNPs form a complex. The presence of the target molecule competes with this complex for binding, leading to separation of the donor from the AuNPs and recovery of fluorescence. In addition, there are energy transfer efficiency change methods, fluorescence lifetime imaging methods, and so on. These methods can be selected and optimized according to the specific needs of the experiment and the characteristics of the target molecules to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity detection of different substances. Through these methods, the application of AuNPs in FRET can be extended to many fields such as life health and safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical applications of AuNPs-mediated FRET sensors in the field of life health
ce:section.ce:para: Biomolecules such as ions, small molecules and enzymes play vital roles in various physiological processes. Normal levels of biomolecules are crucial for the maintenance of homeostasis and health of organism. However, the concentration and activity of a few biomolecules are significantly upregulated in cells and interstitial fluid during the disease occurs, even in the early stages of diseases. Therefore, accurate detection of these molecules is of prime importance in of effective diagnosis and in-depth investigation of specific diseases. Currently, the development of AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors has emerging as a prospective topic in the field of nanodiagnosis. Numerous AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors capable of selectively sensing biomolecules have been used in the screening of biological samples.

Biothiols is a series of biomolecules containing sulfydryl (-SH), such as cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH). Sulfydryl endows biothiols with highly reduction activities and act as scavengers of reactive oxygen species, which play an important role in maintaining redox homeostasis in biosystems [25,26]. Abnormal alterations in biothiol levels are often considered as important indicator for the occurrence of diseases, including inflammations, cancers, and autoimmune diseases, thus making them attractive biomolecules for biosensing. Currently, AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors have been constructed to detect and sense various biothiols in biological samples.

Cys is a non-essential amino acid, which is involved in biological processes such as protein synthesis, metabolism, and detoxification in human body [27,28]. Due the strong coordination of gold-sulfur (Au–S) bond, various AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors have been developed for Cys detection. Dong et al. used the electrostatic force between positively charged carbon dots (CDs) and negatively charged N-acetyl-l-cysteine capped AuNPs (NAC-AuNPs) to build a novel far-red fluorescent FRET fluorescent sensor for detecting l-cysteine (L-Cys). As shown in Fig. 2 , because of the greatly overlapped spectra, the fluorescence of CDs at 539 nm was effectively transferred to NAC capped AuNPs, resulting in an obvious fluorescence at 630 nm. Subsequently, the addition of L-Cys resulted in a desorption, and the interruption of FRET decrease the fluorescence of NAC-AuNPs at 630 nm, while the fluorescence of CDs at 539 nm remained essentially unchanged, thereby achieveing ratiometric fluorescent detection of L-Cys. This ratiometric fluorescent ratio sensor showed superior selectivity for L-Cys over other biomolecules and great potential for further application in the diagnosis of Cys-related diseases [29]. Based on Cys-induced aggregation of AuNPs, Chen et al. developed a carbon QDs (CQDs) using citric acid and urea as raw materials, and found that the addition of AuNPs caused the fluorescence quenching of CQDs. When Cys is further introduced, it will cause AuNPs to aggregate, resulting in the restoration of fluorescence of CQDs-AuNPs. Using CQDs-AuNPs as fluorescent probe, a simple and rapid selective detection method for Cys was established. This method exhibited a satisfactory sensitivity at low concentration of Cys (0.20–4.0 μM), with a detection limit as low as 12 nM [30]. Similarly, Shi et al. constructed a Cys-responsive FRET sensor with CdTe/ZnS CDs and AuNPs for direct detection of Cys [31]. Based on Cys-mediated dispersion strategy, Liu et al. developed a novel Cys-responsive nanosensor consisting of silicon QDs (Si QDs) and AuNPs for the detection of. Due to its low limit of detection (3.5 nM), the method was successfully used to the sensitive detection of Cys in serum and urine [32]. Using tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs) as carriers, Mirsadoughi et al. designed a FRET system by decoration of sulfur-doped CDs and AuNPs for sensitive detection of Cys in serum [33].

Gong et al. constructed a simple prepared and environmental GSH sensor by the integration of Ce-based metal-organic frameworks (MOF) and AuNPs. Due to the extensive overlap with the absorption spectrum of prepared AuNPs, the fluorescence of Ce-MOF was quenched significantly. After addition of GSH, strong coordination of Au–S bond induced the disassembly of Ce-MOF/AuNPs, which obstructed the FRET effect and further restored the fluorescence of Ce-MOF. The unique feature allows sensitive detection of GSH, showing a limit of detection as low as 58 nM [34]. Zhu et al. reported a GSH sensor based on cysteamine-stabilized CdTe QDs and sodium citrate-stabilized AuNPs. Because of the strong electrostatic interaction and effective spectral overlap of CA-CdTe QDs and AuNPs, a highly effective FRET system was designed. The fluorescence of CA-CdTe QDs was strongly quenched in the presence of AuNPs. Under the optimum pH of 5.5, the positive GSH was able to assemble with negative AuNPs through electrostatic interaction, thereby disturbing the FRET effect between CA-CdTe and AuNPs, because the aggregation of AuNPs led to bathochromic-shift of strong absorbance. This method showed a high sensitivity in the detection of GSH with a LOD as low as 3.3 nM [35].

The majority of prevailing fluorescent probes designed for biothiol detection are constituted of small molecule organic compounds. Nonetheless, these fluorescent probes face great challenges such as the propensity of aggregation in aqueous solution, resulting in fluorescence quenching and curtailing their further applicability [36–38]. Due to their good water solubility and high specific surface area, Au nanomaterials emerge as promising carriers for addressing these challenges. Zhang et al. have developed a water-soluble nanosensor for rapid sensing of biothiols based on FRET between AuNPs and small molecule fluorophores (PN). As shown in Fig. 3 , upon addition of biothiols, PN molecules were released from the surface of AuNPs via ligand-exchange displacement based on the strong affinity of Au for thiol groups, leading to a significant increase in fluorescence [39]. In conclusion, AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors have great potential in sensitive fluorescent detection of biothiold in vitro and biological samples.

Amino acids are indispensable nutrients for human physiological functions. The quantitative methods of arginine mainly include colorimetry and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [40,41]. However, the aforementioned techniques are tedious in preparation process and poor accuracy. Currently, fluorescent sensors based on AuNPs have achieved highly accurate amino acid detection.

Wu et al. proposed a novel FRET fluorescent sensor based on utilizing up-conversion phosphor NaYF4 (donor) and AuNPs (acceptor) for the detection of arginine. Due to the electrostatic force, the proximity of two components leads to occurrence of FRET effect, causing fluorescence quenching of NaYF4. The side chain of arginine contains free aminos that can accept protons and carry positive charges. Upon addition of positively charged arginine, the guanidine group of arginine binds to AuNPs, leading to the separation of AuNPs from NaYF4 and yellow fluorescence recovery. This approach exhibited a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of arginine, with a limit of detection as low as 2.9 mM [42].

DNA contains the genetic information necessary to the syntheses of RNA and further expression of proteins, playing the pivotal roles in vital movement of living organisms. In addition, DNA also is the key incentive the incidence of hereditary diseases. Therefore, accurate detection of DNA with special sequence can effectively predict the incidence of hereditary diseases as well as prevent them in time. Currently, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and next generation sequencing (NGS) are the most commonly used nucleic acid detection methods. Despite significant advances, the high cost, time-consuming, and requirements of specialized instruments hinder their further application in nucleic acid detection in public health institutions [43–45]. Notably, AuNPs-based FRET fluorescent sensors is one of the most common strategies for nucleic acid detection.

G-quadruplex (G4) is a quadruplex DNA molecule overexpressed in numerous cancer cells [46]. The secondary structures of G-quadruplex have garnered significant attention due to their involvement in the regulation of gene expression and their association with various diseases, such as cancer and neurological disorders [47–49]. Bilgen et al. developed an AuNPs-azacyanine5 conjugated fluorescence sensor (AuNPs-Aza5) for in vitro detection of G-quadruplex. Fig. 4 A shows the experimental steps and Fig. 4B shows the experimental principle. When citrate-stabilized AuNPs were added to the Aza5 solution, the fluorescence of Aza5 was effectively quenched by the AuNPs through the FRET effect. The positively charged Aza5 was attracted by the negatively charged citrate groups covering the surface of AuNPs, and the Aza5 molecules were adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs. The fluorescence of Aza was restored after the addition of Cys to AuNPs-Aza5, which may be due to the release of Aza5 from the surface of AuNPs after its substitution by Cys. The strong affinity of Cys for AuNPs through the Au–S bond is a well-recognized phenomenon. Further addition of G4 structures to the conjugated probe (AuNPs + Aza5+Cys) resulted in an immediate quenching (1 min) of the recovered fluorescence, which may be due to the binding of Aza5 to G4s. However, the addition of other nucleic acid structures to the system, including single, double or triple nucleic acid structures, showed no sign of fluorescence turn-off, indicating the validity of the assay probes for G -quadruplex detection. Fig. 4C shows the fluorescence profile of the AuNPs-Aza5 probe for detecting G-quadruplex and the luminescence image obtained under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp. Due to the unique feature, AuNPs-Aza5 exhibits a high selectivity of G4 detection with a low limit of detection of 12.66 nM [50].

Longer sequences engender high specificity in nucleic acid detection, attributing the accurate identification of numerous prevalent diseases. Liu et al. constructed a FRET-based sensor that contained NaYF4:Yb,Er nanoparticles as donors and AuNPs as acceptors. The modified NaYF4:Yb,Er nanoparticles that were equipped carboxylic acid groups through poly (acrylic acid) treatment to enhance hydrophilicity, were further functionalized by 5’ end of capture oligonucleotides strand. In the presence of target HIV DNA, AuNPs with report DNA were brought into close proximity to NaYF4:Yb,Er nanoparticles through a “head-to-tail” sandwich hybridization. As shown in Fig. 5 , traditional “tail-to-tail” sandwich structure, donor and acceptor segregated in two sides by target DNA sequence, has no way to achieve an efficient FRET. Compared with the traditional "tail-to-tail" structure, the "head-to-tail" structure significantly improves the efficiency of FRET detection of long-chain HIV target DNA. Moreover, due to the efficient FRET effect and good photostable nanoparticles of upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and AuNPs, the sensitive and specific detection of HIV DNA was achieved with a low LOD of 3 nM51. Furthermore, PNAs are synthetic DNA/RNA analogs in which the entire phosphate sugar backbone is replaced by an uncharged polyamide backbone. The unique backbone properties, especially the charge neutrality, not only give PNA excellent hybridization properties, such as higher binding affinity and better specificity, but also bring about specific interactions with nanomaterials [52]. it has been reported that PNA can induce the immediate aggregation of citrate ion-coated AuNPs, while PNA-DNA complexes can effectively keep the particles dispersed [53,54]. Therefore, Gao et al. developed a fluorescence quantificationsensor based on peptide nucleic acid (PNA)-mediated FRET effect between CDs and AuNPs for the detection of DNA. As shown in Fig. 6 , in the presence of DNA, PNA induced immediate aggregation of AuNPs, thus leading to the recovery of the FRET-quenched fluorescence of CDs. However, the addition of the complementary target DNA can protect AuNPs from being aggregated by forming of DNA/PNA complexes. This resulted in a strong fluorescence quenching of CDs by dispersed AuNPs, with a remarkable LOD of 0.21 nM [7]. This method demonstrated excellent specificity towards fully matched DNA.

Messenger RNA (mRNA) is the key molecule of the central dogma, acting as the transmitter of genetic information that links the cell genotype and its phenotype [55]. The mRNAs family holds great promise as biomarkers for the diagnosis and prediction of various diseases [56,57]. Overs the past decade, there has been a focus on AuNPs-based fluorescent probes for the targeted identification of intracellular RNA species.

Real-time detection and visualization of mRNA concentration and distribution in living cells is of great importance in molecular biology and clinical studies. Wang et al. proposed a multifunctional fluorescent sensor based on oligonucleotide displacement and dual FRET for intracellular mRNA measurement. As shown in Fig. 7 , the sensor synthesizes two AuNPs-based FRET probes using Cy3 or Cy5 as donors. The probes consisted of Cy3 or Cy5 dyes and double-stranded DNA (dsDNA)-bridged AuNPs that quenched the fluorescent dyes by FRET. The target mRNA is able to bind to both FRET probes, and oligonucleotide displacement occurs between the target mRNA and the two probes, resulting in separation of the fluorescent dye from the AuNPs and recovery of fluorescence. Meanwhile, Cy3 and Cy5 were adjacent to each other after oligonucleotide displacement, resulting in energy transfer between Cy3 and Cy5 (Fig. 7A). Therefore, semiquantification and visualization of intracellular mRNAs was achieved by detecting the emission of Cy5 after Cy3 excitation (Fig. 7B). The target mRNA in this method must hybridize with two FRET probes, which improves the specificity of the assay, reduces false positives, is highly resistant to intracellular fluctuations, and is very stable [58].

Due to the short sequences, single base disparities and exceptionally low concentrations of microRNAs (miRNAs), there presents a formidable challenge in their accurate detection in clinical specimens. Somayeh et al. selected CDs and AuNPs as the energy donor-acceptors pair and Rhodamine B (RB) as a signal amplification reagent to prepared an effective miRNA sensor based on FRET effect and RNA hybridization. The probe demonstrated excellent sensitivity and specificity for miRNA-155 with good linearity from 1 aM to 0.1 μM and a low LOD of 0.3 aM [59]. Xiang et al. reported a dual responsive sensor based on a FRET pair of CdTe@CdS QDs and AuNPs for simultaneous detection of hepatocellular carcinoma-associated miRNA-122 and miRNA-199a/b-3p. Due to the excellent sensitivity of the proposed sensor, the LODs for miRNA-122 and miRNA-199a/b-3p were as low as 0.2 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively [60]. Cui et al. utilized CdSeTe/ZnS QDs as a signal reporter and a target recovery strategy as a signal amplifier for the detection of miRNA-155. In the presence of miR155, part of the hairpin probe (HP) immobilized onto the surface of AuNPs@SiO2, could hybridize with it. Then, numerous H1-AuNPs@SiO2 could be produced with the aid of exonuclease III. With the linkage of the assistant chain A2, H1-AuNPs@SiO2 combined with A1-QDs and then the fluorescence signal of QDs was quenched by AuNPs@SiO2 via FRET. The detection limit was as low as 3 aM [61].

Proteins are important functional molecules in living organisms, playing key roles in signaling pathways and biological processes. Protein quantitative analysis is capable of measuring protein concentration and expression level in cells, which is of great significance for biomedical research. Lately, biosensors reliant on FRET effect within nanoscale of donor-acceptor systems that are concomitant with precise molecular recognition occurrences, have garnered substantial interest in the investigation of protein interactions. The unique features allow for quantitative assessment of protein biomarkers with high precision.

Zhou et al. developed a FRET-based homogeneous aptasensor for detection of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP). In the aptasensor, luminescent CdTe QDs were labeled with an AFP aptamer as a fluorescence donor and anti-AFP antibody functionalized AuNPs as a suited energy acceptor. In the presence of AFP, the affinity between the aptamer and the antibody brought the QDs and AuNPs close enough to result in the occurrence of FRET, efficiently quenching the fluorescence of the QDs (Fig. 8 ). Therefore, the aptasensor showed a concentration-dependent fluorescence change in the low concentration of AFP (0.5–45 ng mL−1), with a good linear relationship and a limit of detection as low as 400 pg mL−1 [62]. Duan et al. developed a sensitive and selective metallothioneins (MTs) sensing system based on FRET effect between CQDs and AuNPs. The fluorescence changes demonstrated a good linear relationship in the range of low concentration MTs (12–210 nM), and the limit of detection was 5.25 nM [63]. Somayeh et al. used fluorescent RB-conjugated CDs in a PVA-stabilized hydrogel matrix and AuNPs as acceptors in the FRET process. The RB-CDs’ fluorescence (at 582 nm and 675 nm under 430 nm excitation) is quenched in the presence of AuNPs. Due to the tight coordination between RB-CDs and Amyloid beta peptide (Aβ) antibody, after the addition of Aβ peptide, the ratio of I582/I675 altered with changing the Aβ concentration. The limit of detection was 0.5 pM [64]. Verma et al. reported a convenient, rapid and sensitive sensor by using a FRET pair of rhodamine 6G (R6G) donor and AuNPs acceptor for detection of bovine serum albumin (BSA). When R6G was electrostatically self‐adsorbed to AuNPs surface the fluorescence intensity was quenched. After addition of BSA, the fluorescence intensity of the R6G recovered as BSA induced aggregation of the AuNPs [3]. Similarly, Zhang et al. used mannose-functionalized QDs and mannose-functionalized AuNPs, a FRET assay system was developed and a mannose-specific glycan-binding proteins (GBP) concanavalin A could be detected in 5 min with a detection limit of 3 nM [65].

Enzymes play a pivotal role in maintaining vital movement by participating in various physiological processes within the human body. Their abnormal levels have been associated with the occurrence and development of various diseases, such as gastrointestinal disorders, compromised immune function, and other adverse conditions. Recently, fluorescence analysis has gained great attention in bioassays.

Telomerase, a prominent biomarker in the early stages of cancer, has attracted significant attention for tumor detection and clinical interventions [66,67]. Zhang et al. reported a telomerase-responsive AuNPs sensor. Firstly, AuNPs were functionalized with a telomerase substrate (SH-prime), followed by a 6-carboxy-fluorescein (FAM) modified strand (FAM-probe) to the surface of AuNPs through its complementary stand (SH-attach). The FRET interaction between FAM and AuNPs led to fluorescence quenching. In the presence of telomerase, the SH-primers extended in repeating units (TTAGGG)n on the AuNPs. The extension sequence displaced the FAM strand, restoring a sensitive fluorescence signal with a low limit of detection (0.5 cells μL−1) [68]. Zhang et al. developed a fluorescent sensor for sensitive detection of telomerase by assembling a AuNPs/graphene oxide (AuNPs/GO) probe, which was successfully used for in situ fluorescence imaging of telomerase, inhibitor screening and drug delivery into cells [69].

Trypsin, one of the primary proteases in the pancreas, plays a crucial role in various physiological processes such as cell growth, differentiation and apoptosis [70,71]. Despite the existence of numerous biosensing modalities for trypsin quantification, a substantial number of these approaches require high-cost and intricate instrumentation and protocols. Xu et al. proposed a fluorescence turn-on strategy based on FRET interaction between AuNPs and amino-functionalized CDs for sensitive and selective detection of trypsin. As depicted in Fig. 9 , trypsin selectively hydrolyzes the negatively charged fragment of the peptide, which expose a shorter positively charged peptide, thereby result in the aggregation of the AuNPs by the strong electrostatic interactions between the positively charged peptides and the negatively charged AuNPs. The phenomenon led to the recovery of quenched fluorescence from CDs. The concentration of trypsin can be determined according to the change in fluorescence with a low limit of detection (0.84 ng mL−1) [72]. Wang et al. developed a simple, label-free, and sensitive fluorometric strategy for the detection of trypsin based on FRET effect between sulfhydryl gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and AuNPs via protamine as a bridge. Protamine can trigger the aggregation of AuNPs and link AuNCs with aggregated AuNPs through electrostatic interaction. Compared with monodisperse AuNPs, the UV–vis absorption band of aggregated AuNPs overlapped considerably with the emission spectrum of AuNCs. Thus, the fluorescence of AuNCs was obviously quenched by the aggregated AuNPs through FRET. In the presence of trypsin, protamine was hydrolyzed into small fragments, leading to the deaggregation of AuNPs and breaking of the short distance between AuNPs and AuNCs, so the FRET process was inhibited, and the fluorescence of AuNCs was recovered. The detection limit was 1.9 ng mL−1 [73].

Glutathione S-transferase (GST) is a group of multifunctional enzymes that participates in various physiological processes, such as xenobiotic biotransformation, drug metabolism, and metabolism of deleterious substances [74,75]. Fluorescent conjugated polymer nanoparticles (FCPNPs) represent a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials ccomposed of conjugated polymer monomers with bright fluorescence, owing to their rich π-electron conjugated system. Han et al. have developed a label-free FCPNPs-based single-particle enumeration (SPE) sensor for sensitive detection of GST. As shown in Fig. 10 , the negatively charged FCPNPs-GSH bound to the positively charged GNPs@PEI through electrostatic interactions, inhibiting the fluorescence of FCPNPs-GSH. The number of fluorescent particles on the slide surface observed using total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microimaging was significantly reduced. In the presence of GST, FCPNPs-GSH preferentially bound to GST because of the specific interaction between FCPNPsGSH and GST, and the fluorescence of FCPNPs-GSH was restored and the fluorescent particles were significantly enhanced. The sensor showed a good linear range of low GST concentration (0.01–6 μg/mL) and a low LOD of 1.03 ng mL−1. This research presents a promising approach for sensitive and selective GST [76].

Arginine kinase (AK) is one of the most important allergen found in shrimp products [77]. AK possesses regulatory and transport functionalities, playing a crucial role in the metabolism of invertebrates and catalyzing ATP production [78]. Wang et al. developed a novel immunoassay sensor for the quantitation of AK, by integrating AuNPs and antibody-modified QDs. As shown in Fig. 11 , when the first antibody-modified AuNPs (AuNPs- Ab1) are bridged with the second antibody-modified QDs (QDs- Ab2) via AK, FRET occurs between the AuNPs and the QDs resulting in a decrease in fluorescent signal. The decrease in fluorescence is logarithm proportionate to AK concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−6-1.0 × 10−3 mg/mL, with a low limit of detection (0.11 ng mL−1). This method provides a highly sensitive and selective approach for AK detection [79].

Noble metal nanoclusters (MNCs) represent a novel class of fluorophores that have attracted considerable attention as captivating alternatives to inorganic QDs and organic dyes, owing to their intriguing electronic, physical, chemical and catalytic properties. Adenosine deaminase (ADA) is distributed in all tissues of human body and is crucial for intracellular and extracellular purine metabolism [80]. Dysregulation of ADA is closely associated with many malignant diseases [81]. Wang et al. constructed a split aptamer-based FRET platform using AuNCs and AuNPs for the detection of ADA. As shown in Fig. 12 , the adenosine triphosphate (ATP) aptamer was divided into two fragments (P1 and P2). the amino group of P1 reacted with the carboxyl group of AuNCs via condensation to form P1–AuNCs, and P2 adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs via Au–S bond to form P2-AuNPs coupler. In the presence of ATP, ATP binds to two high-affinity fragments, linking P1–AuNCs and P2-AuNPs together, whereby the fluorescence of P1–AuNCs is quenched from P1–AuNCs to P2-AuNPs by FRET. Upon addition of ADA, ATP is converted to inosine triphosphate (ITP), which has no affinity for the aptamer, releasing P1 and P2 and restoring system fluorescence. The signals of the fluorescent platform showed a good linear relationship with ADA concentration in the range of 2–120 U L−1 with a low LOD of 0.72 U L−1 [82].

Furthermore, Zhan et al. constructed a fast, label-free, and highly sensitive fluorescent biosensor for effective detection of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity using FRET effect between boron nitride QDs (BNQDs) and AuNPs, which showed a low LOD of 0.0212 mU/mL. As shown in Fig. 13 , the hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine (ATCh) by AChE to generate thiocholine (TCh) reduced HAuCl4 to AuNPs and formed TCh-BNQDs/AuNPs aggregates in situ, leading to fluorescence quenching of BNQDs. In addition, the organophosphorus pesticide paraoxon reduced AChE activity and hindered the enzymatic reaction, thereby attenuating the fluorescence quenching of BNQDs [83]. Li et al. developed a fluorescent ligand sensor for the trace detection of thrombin based on FRET effect between CdS QDs and AuNPs, achieving a low of 0.38 nM L−1 [84]. Karimi et al. achieved sensitive detection of methyltransferase by utilizing the fluorescence activation of FAM-labeled DNA quenched by AuNPs, showing a low LOD of 2.2 pM [85].
The reported works of AuNPs-based fluorescent sensors for biomolecular sensing are summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical applications of AuNPs-mediated FRET sensors in the field of life safety
ce:section.ce:para: FRET technology based on AuNPs shows great potential in the field of food safety and environmental pollutant detection. The presence of pesticides, bacteria, viruses, and environmental pollutants poses a serious threat to human life safety and ecosystems. In order to safeguard public safety and health, it is crucial to develop highly sensitive and selective detection methods. Currently, FRET sensors that are highly sensitive to specific microorganisms or pollutants can be designed by precisely modulating the size, shape, and surface chemistry of AuNPs. These sensors are capable of quantitatively analyzing the concentration of target molecules by monitoring the energy transfer efficiency between donor and acceptor.

As a result of advances in agricultural practices and increased global demand for cereals, vegetables and fruits, the levels of pesticides in agricultural products have frequently increased, with deleterious effects on human well-being. AuNPs-based fluorescence techniques facilitate the selective and sensitive detection of pesticide in various sample at nanomole concentrations and even lower.

Notably, organophosphorus (OP) and carbamate pesticides are capable of targeting serine residue within the active site of AChE, therefore inhibiting the natural activity and function of AChE [86]. Jyoti et al. reported AChE responsive FRET sensor by utilizing CQDs as donors and AuNPs as acceptors. In this sensor, acetylthiocholine (ATCh) is a specific substrate of AChE, which is underwent catalytic hydrolysis by high levels of AChE to generates thiocholine, further instigating the aggregation of AuNPs. In the presence of pesticides, the fluorescence of CQDs were quenched due to inhibition of catalytic activity of AChE, which facilitated the detection of pesticides. The fluorescent sensor exhibited high sensitivity in pesticide detection, achieving remarkable LOD: 0.05 nM (paraoxon), 0.10 nM (malathion), 0.12 nM (methamidophos), and 0.13 nM (carbaryl) [87].

Thiourea (TU), a sulfur-containing organic compound, has become a significant concern due to the widespread pollution in soil, river, and even fruits, posing risks to both the environment and human health [88,89]. Hu et al. designed a fluorescent sensor using carbon nanodots (CNDs) and AuNPs probes for TU detection, achieving a low LOD of 3.62 × 10−8 M. This sensor had various merits such as easy to operate, good selectivity and sensitivity, capable of detecting TU in environmental samples and the compound fruit and vegetable juices [90]. In addition, Liu et al. established a fluorescent sensor based on the FRET interaction between AuNPs and CdTe QDs for the detection of triadimenol in tap water and agricultural irrigation water, which showed a low LOD of 0.182 mM [91].

Accurate detection of pathogenic microorganisms is critical for the diagnosis infections. Recently, AuNPs-based FRET sensors have emerged as a potent tool for highly sensitive detection of bacteria and viruses, holding great potential for their applications in clinical settings.

The public health risk posed by bacterial infections has prompted an imperative for the development of faster and reliable methods for bacteria detection. Microbial pathogens, such as Escherichia coli (E. coli) pose multifaceted threats to human being, propelling the innovation of efficient strategies in microbiological detection, which span various fields, including food safety, medical diagnostics and water quality [92–95]. Zhang et al. developed a versatile dual-responsive sandwich sensor utilizing a FRET pair, comprising specific aptamer-modified QDs (Aptamer-QDs) and lectin-bean protein A (Con A)-modified AuNPs (Con A-AuNPs). The innovative sensor enabled rapid and sensitive detection of E. coli within just 0.5 h. As illustrated in Fig. 14 , the close proximity of aptamer-QDs and Con A-AuNPs induced discernible changes in fluorescence signals. The constructed FRET sensor exhibited a linear detection range for E. coli detection (102 - 2 × 108 cfu/mL), with a low LOD of 45 cfu mL−1 [96]. This sensor, characterized by its sensitivity, holds promise for applications in the detection of pathogenic bacteria. Ghayyem et al. used azithromycin-functionalized CeO2 nanoparticles (Azm-CeO2NPs) as energy donors and aptamer-modified AuNPs (aptamer-AuNPs) as energy acceptors for the detection of E. coli. The binding of Azm and aptamer on the surface of E. coli facilitated the construction of FRET system directly on the bacteria surface. The novel sensor enabled the detection of E. coli within approximately 30 min, meanwhile showing a broad linear range, excellent specificity and a low LOD of 1.04 cfu mL−1 [97]. Moreover, this strategy with high reliability has emerged as a potent tool for the detection of E. coli in real samples. Heli et al. fabricated a novel and versatile immunosensor on a nanopaper platform for the detection of E. coli. Energy transfer occurred between the Alexa Fluor 488 and plasmonic nanopaper, where CBC embedded with AuNPs (AuNPs-CBC) served as the acceptor. The process was depended on the interaction between the antibody and bacteria and the conformational transition of the antibody. Based this sensor, E. coli (DH5α) was reliably detected at concentrations as low as 50 CFU mL−1 [98]. Saad et al. reported a sensitive sensor using a target oligos-jointed FRET pair of CDs and AuNPs for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 [99].

Vibrio parahaemolyticus (V. parahaemolyticus), a gram-negative bacterium thriving in saline environments, is mainly distributed in coastal areas [100,101]. The consumption of food contaminated V. parahaemolyticus can result in symptoms such as abdominal pain, vomiting, and watery stools, with severe cases potentially leading to fatality [102,103]. Ren et al. proposed FRET-based paper sensors for quantitative detection of V. parahaemolyticus using smartphones. As shown in Fig. 15 , the sensor design involved a double-stranded DNA (aptamer- AuNPs probe) that formed by the hybridization of aptamers with AuNPs. Capture probes in this strategy were prepared by chemically coupling CDs to amino-modified single-stranded DNA. Upon exposure to V. parahaemolyticus, the aptamer within the double-stranded DNA bound specifically to the target bacterium, activating the sensor and releasing the AuNPs to bind to the capture probe. The FRET interaction between CDs and AuNPs induced a noticeable change in the fluorescence of CDs from bright blue to colorless. This novel sensor holds promise for efficient and portable detection of V. parahaemolyticus [104].

Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium) is a causative agent of global bacterial food poisoning events, entailing substantial economic burdens associated with surveillance, investigation, treatment, and preventive measure. Consequently, the rapid and accurate detection of S. typhimurium remains a focal point of food safety. Srinivasan et al. designed a rapid sensor utilizing a FRET pair of RB and AuNPs for the detection S. typhimurium. In this approach, the aptamer adsorbs to the AuNP surface, which protect them from salt-induced aggregation. This interaction led to the fluorescence quenching of RB in presence of AuNPs. Upon the addition of S. typhimurium, the bacteria specifically bound to the aptamer, diminishing the stability of AuNPs and causing the aggregation of AuNPs. This process resulted in the fluorescence recovery of RB and demonstrated a low LOD for S. typhimurium of 464 cfu mL−1 [105].

Ouyang et al. developed a novel sensor utilizing aptamer recognition for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). In this sensor, aptamer-functionalized AuNPs (AuNPs-aptamers) acted as the acceptor, while UCNPs as the donor. The interaction involved the binding of AuNPs-aptamers with cDNA-modified UCNPs (UCNPs-cDNA) by complementary pairing, resulting in efficient fluorescence quenching. Upon treated with S. aureus, the aptamers specifically combined with S. aureus, causing the dissociation the nanosystem and the recovery of fluorescence. The sensor demonstrated a commendable detection limit of 10.7 cfu mL−1, highlighting its potential for sensitive detection of S. aureus [106].

Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) is an acute infectious illness caused by SARS-CoV-2, characterized by predominant lung symptoms and potential damage to the digestive and nervous system [107]. Li et al. successfully enggineered fluorescent graphene QDs (GQDs) and Ag@AuNPs for diagnostic purposes. FRET interaction between the two parts enabled effective quenching of GQDs fluorescence. In the presence of 2019-nCoV monoclonal antibody, the interaction between Ag@AuNPs-NCP (2019-nCoV antigen) complexes and GQDs was impeded due to site-blocking, restoring the fluorescence of GQDs. The method showed a low LOD of 50 fg mL−1, holding great potential for the detection of SARS-CoV-2 [108].

Infectious bursal disease virus (IBDV) stands as a significant contributor to frequent occurrences in global poultry industry [109,110]. Sabzehparvar et al. reported a novel sensor utilizing two oligonucleotide probes, that labeled with QDs and rhodamine-immobilized AuNPs (AuNPs-Rh) for the detection of IBDV VP2 gene. Two amino- and thiol-modified oligonucleotides specific to the VP2 gene were immobilized on the surface of the QDs and AuNPs, respectively. In the presence of target marker, AuNPs-Rh will be located in the surface of the QDs, trigging FRET interaction and changes in fluorescence. The method exhibited ultralow LOD of 30 nM for IBDV VP2 gene [111].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food safety and environmental pollution detection
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, increased attention has been directed on the crucial concerns in food safety and environmental pollution. Consequently, fluorescence techniques have become increasingly popular in the detection of food and environmental pollutants, due to their merits of trace analysis and high sensitivity.

Mycotoxins, a category of natural poisons, pose a global public health challenge. Given its widespread implications, quantification of toxins is a crucial research in food safety. Among various toxins, trichothecene mycotoxin (T-2) is one of the most toxic food contaminants and is even considered a potential biological weapon [112,113]. Even at very low doses, T-2 can permeate the skin, leading to acute and subacute lethal effects on both animal and human with prolonged exposure [114]. As shown in Fig. 16 , Khan et al. synthesized a green-emitting l-arginine@6-aza-2-thiothymine-protected gold nanoclusters (Arg@ATT-AuNCs). Then, PAA@Arg@at-AuNCs nanoparticles (NPs) were constructed by coating Arg@ATT-AuNCs on polyacrylic acid (PAA). The electrostatic interaction of aptamer-PDDA complexes prevents the aggregation of AuNPs, and AuNPs (520 nm absorbance) were able to quench the fluorescence of PAA@Arg@ATT-AuNCs NPs (530 nm emission). The addition of T-2 resulted in toxin aptamer assembly, allowing subsequent aggregation of AuNPs with a bathochromic-shift in absorbance to 665 nm. The process recover the fluorescence at 530 nm with a LOD for T-2 as low as 0.57 pg mL−1. Spiking analysis revealed the robustness and efficiency of the sensor in the standard ELISA method [115].

Aflatoxin, a metabolite produced by Aspergillus aflatus and Aspergillus parasiticus under humid and hot environment [116]. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is the most toxic mycotoxin and is prevalent in various agricultural products [117]. Xiong et al. reported a rapid and sensitive sensor using an electrostatic-mediated FRET effect for the detection of AFB1. As shown in Fig. 17 , in the presence of AFB1, the aptamer preferentially binds to AFB1, NaCl induces the aggregation of AuNPs, and the AuNPs solution changes from burgundy to purple, and this color change is used for colorimetric channel analysis. Meanwhile, positively charged quantum dots were introduced into the reaction system to interact with negatively charged AuNPs, which successfully converted the color signal into a more sensitive fluorescence signal by FRET. With the increase of AFB1 concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased and the fluorescence of the aptamer sensor was gradually restored. The dual-readout unmodified ligand sensor designed in this study provides a promising method for simple and accurate detection of other mycotoxins in food [118]. In a similar approach, Lu et al. develop a target-driven switched fluorescent aptamer sensor for trace detection of AFB1. The fluorescence the CdZnTe QDs was effectively quenched because the hybridization reaction brought CdZnTe QDs and AuNPs. The incubation of AFB1 led to the formation of aptamer-AFB1 complex, separating the donor-acceptor complex and subsequent enabling the target-driven switch-on fluorescence of CdZnTe QDs for the quantitative detection of AFB1 [119]. Li et al. reported a FRET hairpin probe used CdTe/CdS QDs as donor and AuNPs as acceptor for aflatoxin gene detection. In the absence of the target DNA, the sensor is non-fluorescent due to the FRET-mediated fluorescence quenching. Upon addition of the target DNA, the specific interaction between the target and the hairpin probe switch led to the separation of the fluorescent donor-acceptor pair, resulting in an increasing fluorescence response for the ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin gene (LOD = 0.02 nM) [120].

In addition, Serebrennikova et al. used fluorophores (AMF) and AuNPs as a FRET pair to construct fluorescent sensor for the detection of ochratoxin a (OTA). Based on competitive immunoreaction between free OTA and OTA-AMF for binding to anti-OTA antibodies labeled with AuNPs, the sensor exhibited a recovery of OTA-AMF fluorescence after addition of OTA, showing a LOD of 0.02 ng mL−1 [121].

Food additives are employed in food processing to improve product quality and extend shelf life. However, the abuse of harmful substances as food additives is strictly prohibited due to their harmful properties. Benzoyl peroxide (BPO) is widely used as a food additive in the food industry due to its bleaching and disinfection properties [122,123]. Researches have shown that BPO could be a potential tumor inducer and it might increase the risk of cancer [124]. Deng et al. constructed a sensitive fluorescent nanoplatform using CDs-GSH coated AuNPs (GSH-AuNPs) system for the detection of BPO. GSH oxidation caused by BPO contributed to the accumulation of AuNPs in concentrated salt solution, resulting a change in fluorescence and low LOD for BPO (0.1 μg g−1) [125]. Zhang et al. constructed a FRET sensing system based on conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) and AuNPs for the detection of melamine. The fluorescence of CPNs were efficiently quenched by AuNPs, and the aggregation of AuNPs caused by N–Au interaction interrupted the FRET effect and promoted the fluorescence of CPNs. The system is high selectivity and sensitivity for melamine detection, with a LOD as low as 1.7 nM [126]. Qian et al. developed a FRET aptamer sensor based on CDs and AuNPs for the detection of 17β-estradiol. Upon addition of 17β-estradiol, the aptamer specifically bound to 17β-estradiol, resulting in the disruption of the FRET system between CDs and AuNPs. This method demonstrated a remarkable sensitivity, achieving a low LOD of 245 pM [127]. Li et al. developed an aptamer-based FRET sensor for the detection of 17β-estradiol. The fluorescence of thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe QDs (TGA-CdTe QDs) was significantly quenched by cysteamine-AuNPs due to the FRET interaction. Upon addition of 17β-estradiol, the FRET interaction was dynamically decreased, because the specific recognition between 17β-estradiol and aptamers allowed the aptamers-mediated aggregation of cysteamine-AuNP. Notably, the method achieved a highly sensitive detection with a low LOD of 0.057 ng mL−1 [128].

Mousavizadeh et al. fabricated a simple and fast dual-mode nanosensor for the colorimetric and fluorimetric detection of gluten. Distance-dependent colorimetric and fluorescence phenomena were involved in the design of dual-mode nanosensor. In the presence of gluten proteins, the inter-particle distance between AuNPs and crystalline nano cellulose (CNCs) was enlarged, with significantly recovery of the fluorescence of CNCs [129]. Dong et al. reported a sensitive Cartap-responsive sensor based on a FRET pair of AuNPs and RB. In the presence of citrate-stabilized AuNPs, the fluorescence of RB was remarkably quenched by AuNPs due to the FRET interaction. Upon addition of cartap, the fluorescence of the AuNPs-RB system was remarkable recovered, due to the absorbance of cartap with two amino on the surface of AuNPs [130].

In general, a large number of sensors selected AuNPs as key components to detect heavy metals rely on the interaction of ligands located on the surface of nanoparticles. Liu et al. have reported a nanosensor based on FRET interaction between UCNPs and short-chain aptamer-functionalized AuNPs for the detection of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 18 , the nanosensor consists of two aptamers, which are specific to Hg2+ and short-chain, respectively. In the absence of Hg2+, the two aptamers were matched to each other, leading to fluorescence quenched of UCNPs by FRET interaction between UCNPs and AuNPs. In the presence of Hg2+, the long-chain aptamer folds into a hairpin structure due to high affinity between Hg2+ and thymine, releasing of AuNPs from UCNPs and resulting in fluorescence recovery. This nanosensor exhibits good selectivity and has a low LOD of 60 nM [6]. Amiri et al. reported a fluorescent sensor based on a FRET pair of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) modified CDs and complementary DNA (cDNA) modified AuNPs for Hg2+ detection. Based on selective interaction of Hg2+ ions with thymine (T) in CDs-ssDNA, the aptasensor is able to detection Hg2+ through the changes in the fluorescence [131]. Hamd-Ghadareh et al. synthesized amine-functionalized CDs, which were subsequently coupled to RB-CDs and thymine-rich ssDNA(S1) component on the surface of CDs, thereby achieving dual channels optical behavior. In the present of AuNPs-cDNA (S2), the produced RB-CDs-ssDNA was able to successfully detect Hg2+ [132]. Zhou et al. developed a simple and high-sensitive method for mercury determination based on the FRET between AuNPs and R6G. The fluorescence intensity of R6G was markedly quenched when R6G was attached to the surface AuNPs. Upon addition of Hg2+ in the AuNPs- R6G system, the effective reaction of Hg2+ with AuNPs and failure of FRET complex, which results in R6G with a further distance from AuNPs and the increase of fluorescence intensity of R6G [133].

Li et al. developed an economical, simple, and sensitive fluorescence sensor based on FRET interaction between CQDs and AuNPs for Pb2+ detection. GSH-functionalized CQDs acted as both fluorescent donor and specific adsorbent to Pb2+ via Pb-GSH complexes. The absorbance of Pb2+ on the surface of CQDs facilitated the further reaction with –SH group of AuNPs to form sandwich Au–PdS-CQDS, which results in a significant decrease in fluorescence of CQDs. The sensor achieved sensitive detection of Pb2+ with a LOD as low as 4.1 × 10−8 mg mL−1 [134]. Cai et al. prepared a novel nanocomposite Fe3O4@Au-FITC for fluorescence detection of Pb2+. The Fe3O4@AuNPs–FITC nanocomposites could be etched by Pb2+ in the presence of Na2S2O3, leading to fluorescence recovery of FITC quenched by Fe3O4@Au nanocomposites. Fe3O4@Au-FITC achieved sensitive Pb2+ detection with an untra low LOD of 5.2 nM [135]. Sun et al. designed a bi-functional nanosensor for the detection of Cr3+ and sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP). As shown in Fig. 19 , in the presence of Cr3+, the monodispersed AuNPs aggregated rapidly, which leads to the red-shift of the absorption of AuNPs and quenches the red emission of UCNPs. In the presence of STPP, the formation of Cr3+-STPP complex weakens the aggregation of AuNPs and the red emission of UCNPs was increased. In the bi-functional nanosensor, LOD of Cr3+ and STPP were 0.44 μM and 0.54 μM, respectively [136].

In addition, Devi et al. prepared AuNPs using sodium citrate as a reductant and mercaptoethylamine (MEA) as a coating agent in an aqueous solution. Amine-functionalized AuNP@MEA were used as quenchers to quench the fluorescence of QDs. Trinitrotoluene (TNT), characterized as an electron-deficient species, is tend to bind with the electron-rich amines. The interaction of TNT with amine groups present on the AuNPs results in recovering of the QDs fluorescence. The detection limit of 21.9 nM [137].

Table 2 summarizes the reports on fluorescent sensors based on AuNPs, demonstrating the application of these sensors in the detection of different types of food safety and environmental pollutants. These studies indicate that AuNPs-based FRET technology has a wide range of applications in the field of food safety and environmental monitoring, providing powerful technical support for disease diagnosis, food safety assurance and environmental protection. With the deepening of the research, more powerful and easy-to-operate AuNPs-FRET sensors are expected to be developed in the future to meet the increasing demand for detection.

In recent times, nanomaterials have significantly expanded the scope of FRET applications in detection. Among these, AuNPs are frequently utilized as fluorescence quenchers in FRET due to their broad absorption band in the visible region and large molar extinction coefficient. In this review, we discuss the energy transfer mechanism of AuNPs as receptors in FRET systems and summarize the advances made in the development of AuNP-based FRET biosensors for the analytical detection of some biomolecules, pesticides, enzymes, microorganisms, food safety and environmental pollutants in the areas of life health and security. Compared with other nanomaterials, AuNPs exhibit excellent size-dependent quenching effect, efficient surface plasmon resonance (SPR) enhancement effect, good chemical and thermal stability, and outstanding biocompatibility as FRET receptors [138–140]. For example, the fluorescence quenching ability of AuNPs is usually stronger compared to two-dimensional nanomaterials such as transition metal sulfides (e.g., MoS2) because the SPR effect of AuNPs produces a strong concentration of electromagnetic fields at the surface of AuNPs, whereas the two-dimensional materials may not have this localized electromagnetic field enhancement effect [141]. In addition, the advantages of AuNPs in fluorescence quenching applications are further enhanced by their well-established synthesis and surface modification methods, which are easy to realize customized designs, which provide flexibility for designing FRET probes with specific functions [142,143].

Although research indicate that AuNPs hold great potential as effective FRET agents. There are still some limitations to practical applications that need to be solved, and further investigation into the underlying mechanisms is required. For instance, Most AuNPs exhibit a higher affinity for molecules containing thiol groups. Therefore, addressing the issue of non-specific adsorption of AuNPs with non-target biomolecules, which can lead to the generation of false positive signals, poses a significant challenge for the selectivity and accuracy of sensors. Additionally, the large specific surface area of AuNPs limits the exposure of attached targeting molecules, which may reduce the sensitivity and detection efficiency of the FRET sensor. Thirdly, through AuNPs have exhibited excellent biocompatibility and biosafety, The biocompatibility and biological effects of AuNPs-based FRET biosensors should be further explored, especially when they are applied to in vivo bioanalysis. Herein, although AuNPs as FRET reagents have been extensively reported, there remain numerous unexplored opportunities still exist for their biological applications.
a)
Most of the literature reports on the use of AuNPs utilizing their surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect as energy acceptors in the construction of FRET biosensors. However, the emergence of small-sized Au nanocluster materials has made it possible for them to act as energy donors. Au nanoclusters are considered the most promising nanoparticles due to their atomically precise structure, high biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties from the ultraviolet to the second near-infrared (NIR-II) region. This makes them potential candidates for use as FRET biosensors to facilitate in vivo analysis.
b)
The structure-activity relationship of AuNPs themselves in the construction of FRET biosensors needs further exploration. For instance, researches have shown that the shape of AuNPs, such as spherical, rod-like, star-shaped, etc., can affect their optical properties and will interact with donor molecules. Therefore, a deep understanding of their structure-activity relationship is of great significance for the further development of related FRET sensors.
c)
AuNPs are considered as the most promising materials for biomedical applications due to their excellent biocompatibility and biosafety. Therefore, they are highly suitable for constructing FRET sensors at the in vivo level. For instance, by deploying MMP-responsive biosensors in vivo, the detection of disease-related enzymes can be achieved using fluorescence signals or catalytically generated color changes in urine [144]. This approach can be utilized for the rapid detection of various disease-related proteases, including those associated with infectious diseases. Simultaneously, through personalized design, it is possible to achieve dynamic observation of signals from intracellular target analytes.
d)
Integrating FRET biosensors with imaging modalities like CT, along with harnessing the optical and photothermal conversion properties of AuNPs, facilitates the construction of a diagnosis-therapy integration platform. This will provide real-time monitoring tools for disease diagnosis and treatment.
e)
In addition, there are also drug interaction and pharmacodynamics studies utilizing the FRET quenching property of AuNPs to provide rapid screening methods for new drug development.

In conclusion, the application of AuNPs-based in FRET shows significant potential and development prospects. We believe that more precise and specific FRET probes will appear in the future to meet the growing urgent needs in biomedical areas such as complex sample analysis, clinical diagnosis and therapy.

Xuemei Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yunjing Luo: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yong Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Ziqi Xie: Investigation. Chao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Convenient and ultra-sensitive fluorescence detection of bovine serum albumin by using Rhodamine-6G modified gold nanoparticles in biological samples
